{{Short description="SI base unit of mass}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Kilogram
| image = Prototype kilogram replica.JPG
| caption = Replica of the [[International Prototype of the Kilogram]] (IPK), the former definition of the kilogram.
| topics = [[Mass]], [[International System of Units]], [[Planck constant]]
| unit_system = [[SI base unit]]
| unit_of = [[Mass]]
| symbol = kg
}}

The '''kilogram''' (symbol: '''kg''') is the [[SI base unit]] of [[mass]]. It is widely used in science, engineering, and commerce worldwide. The kilogram is defined by taking the fixed numerical value of the [[Planck constant]] <math>h</math> as 6.62607015×10⁻³⁴ when expressed in the unit J·s (= kg·m²·s⁻¹).

== Definition ==
Since 20 May 2019, the kilogram is defined via the [[Planck constant]]:

:<math>1\ \text{kg} = \frac{h}{6.62607015 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}}</math>

This replaced the [[International Prototype of the Kilogram]] (IPK), a platinum–iridium cylinder stored in [[Sèvres]], France, which had been the standard since 1889.

== Historical definitions ==
* 1795 — defined as the mass of one litre of water at the melting point of ice (the "grave")
* 1799 — the ''Kilogramme des Archives'', a platinum prototype
* 1889 — the IPK adopted by the [[General Conference on Weights and Measures|CGPM]]
* 2019 — redefined in terms of the Planck constant

== Multiples and conversions ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Submultiples !! Multiples
|-
| milligram (mg) = 10⁻⁶ kg || megagram (Mg) = 10³ kg = 1 [[tonne]]
|-
| microgram (μg) = 10⁻⁹ kg || gigagram (Gg) = 10⁶ kg
|}

* 1 kg ≈ 2.20462 [[pound (mass)|pounds]]
* 1 kg = 1000 [[gram]]s

== Significance in physics ==
The kilogram appears in many fundamental equations:
* [[Newton's second law]]: <math>F = ma</math> → kg·m/s² = N
* [[Kinetic energy]]: <math>E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2</math> → kg·m²/s² = J
* [[Momentum]]: <math>p = mv</math> → kg·m/s
* [[Newton's law of universal gravitation]]: <math>F = Gm_1m_2/r^2</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Gram]]
* [[International System of Units]]
* [[Mass]]
* [[Newton (unit)]]
* [[Planck constant]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Kilogram| ]]
[[Category:SI base units]]
[[Category:Units of mass]]
